name: Mud Flood Theory
description: The Mud Flood Theory is a pseudoscientific conspiracy theory claiming that a cataclysmic mud flood event, possibly in the 19th century, drastically altered the landscape and buried the remnants of a prior, advanced civilization. Proponents suggest that evidence of this event can be found in the architecture of older buildings, particularly those featuring 'buried' windows and doors, inconsistencies in historical records, and geological formations. They argue that the official historical narrative is a deliberate cover-up. The theory gained traction online, particularly through platforms like YouTube and alternative history forums. Mainstream historians and geologists dismiss the Mud Flood Theory as lacking credible evidence. They attribute the architectural features cited by proponents to common building practices, such as basements, rising street levels over time, and variations in architectural styles. Geological features are explained by well-understood natural processes like sedimentation, erosion, and geological uplift. Critics point to the absence of substantial archaeological or geological evidence to support a widespread, recent mud flood of the magnitude claimed by proponents. The theory often intertwines with other conspiracy theories, including those related to Tartaria, Freemasonry, and suppressed technologies, further distancing it from mainstream acceptance.
subtitle: Cataclysmic mud event altered history?

claims.claim: A global mud flood occurred in the recent past (19th century or earlier).
claims.evidence: Buried windows and doors in old buildings, inconsistencies in historical records, geological anomalies.
claims.criticism: Buried architectural features are explained by basements, rising street levels, and architectural styles. Historical inconsistencies are attributed to misinterpretations or lack of full records. Geological anomalies have natural explanations.

claims.claim: An advanced civilization existed before the mud flood and was destroyed or buried by it.
claims.evidence: Ornate architecture of pre-20th century buildings, suggesting advanced engineering skills.
claims.criticism: The architecture can be explained by known historical construction techniques and the skills of artisans of the time. No credible evidence of a technologically advanced pre-flood civilization has been found.

claims.claim: The historical narrative is a deliberate cover-up to hide the mud flood event and the prior civilization.
claims.evidence: Lack of clear explanations for certain historical events, discrepancies in historical accounts.
claims.criticism: The alleged discrepancies are often based on misinterpretations or incomplete understanding of historical sources. There is no evidence of a widespread conspiracy among historians and scientists to conceal the truth.

keyFigures.name: Philipp Druzhinin
keyFigures.role: Popularizer of the theory on YouTube.

keyFigures.name: Anatoly Fomenko
keyFigures.role: His 'New Chronology' is sometimes cited to support timelines.

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Prominent skeptic who critiques conspiracy theories

timeline.event: Alleged time of the Mud Flood event.
Emergence and spread of the theory online.

sources.author: Jason Colavito
sources.work: Knowing Fear: Science, Knowledge, and the Development of the Horror Genre
sources.title: Jason Colavito
